tip: Popular personal color diagnosis studio in Hongdae with English-speaking consultants. Book via Klook or their website 2+ weeks ahead — walk-ins not accepted. Sessions run ~60–90 minutes. Chinese and Japanese also available.

Gangnam-based studio with detailed personal color + body type consultation. English available. Book via Kakao Channel or Naver. Sessions are thorough — budget 90 minutes. Price includes a personalized color swatch book to take home.
Budget-friendly personal color studio near Hongdae. English support available — mention when booking. Slightly faster sessions (45–60 min) at a lower price point. Good first-timer option.

Aggregator studio offering personal color across multiple locations. English-friendly booking via seoulpalette.cc. Compare studios by price, area, and language before booking. Good for comparing options in one place.

Korea's largest Olive Young flagship — 3-story mega store opened 2026. 22 checkout registers for minimal wait. Instant tax refund at counter for purchases over ₩15,000 (bring your physical passport). Multilingual signage. Best time: Olive Young sale (올영세일) in March, June, September, December.

Bustling Hongdae location popular with younger tourists. Tax refund available (instant for purchases over ₩15,000). Staff may speak less English than Myeongdong but Korean beauty trends are heavily stocked here.
Large Gangnam Station location. Tax refund available. Less tourist-oriented than Myeongdong — mostly local shoppers. Great for discovering what Koreans actually buy vs tourist-marketed products.

Korea's biggest Daiso — 12 floors of everything from K-beauty dupes to cute stationery. Most items ₩500–₩5,000. Foreigners love the skincare tools, sheet masks, and BTS-themed merch. No tax refund but prices are already rock-bottom. Cash recommended for faster checkout.

Culinary Class Wars White Spoon chef Choi Hyun-seok's restaurant in Cheongdam. Modern Korean-French fusion. Reserve via CatchTable — slots open 2 weeks ahead and fill quickly. No English menu but staff can explain dishes. Lunch is easier to book than dinner.

Legendary Korean-Chinese chef from Culinary Class Wars. Famous for jjajangmyeon (black bean noodles) and tangsuyuk (sweet & sour pork). Walk-in possible for lunch but expect queues. No English menu — point at photos or say 'jjajangmyeon'.

Culinary Class Wars runner-up Edward Lee's Korean-Southern fusion buffet. English spoken fluently. Located in Itaewon — most foreigner-friendly of the CCW restaurants. Reserve via CatchTable. The fried chicken with gochujang glaze is a must-order.

Michelin-starred Korean BBQ in Majang-dong. The ultimate Hanwoo beef experience. Reserve via CatchTable — slots released first day of each month and sell out in minutes. Casual floor is easier to book than the omakase counter. English-speaking staff available.

Michelin-starred temple food restaurant near Insadong. Run by a Buddhist nun — entirely vegan. Set menu changes seasonally. Reserve via CatchTable or call. English menu available. A uniquely Korean dining experience — peaceful, mindful, zero waste.

Seoul's most foreigner-famous jjimjilbang next to Yongsan Station. 8 floors of saunas, baths, sleeping zones, and a rooftop pool. Entry ₩13,000 for 4 hours. English signage throughout. Bring your own toiletries. Tattoos are generally accepted here (unlike some jimjilbangs). Open 24 hours.

Korea's most luxurious jjimjilbang inside Shinsegae Centum City (world's largest department store). Premium facilities with 22 themed sauna rooms. 4-hour limit on entry. English signage. More expensive than local jimjilbangs but worth it — like a spa resort.

Most popular hanbok rental chain near Gyeongbokgung. Booking via Klook gives discounts. Includes hairstyling and accessories. Wearing hanbok = FREE entry to all Seoul palaces. Rent for 4 hours to comfortably explore the palace + Bukchon. Avoid Tuesdays — palace is closed.

Korea's most famous dance studio — home to K-pop choreographers for BTS, TWICE, Blackpink. Open classes daily. Book via 1milliondance.com — English site available. Drop-in friendly. All levels welcome. Bring indoor sneakers. No filming during class but practice videos encouraged after.

Overnight temple stay at Seoul's main Buddhist temple. Includes meditation, tea ceremony, 108 prostrations, and temple food. English programs available — book at templestay.com. Wear comfortable loose clothing. Wake-up is at 4am for morning chanting — an unforgettable experience.

The most foreigner-friendly mall in Dongdaemun. Open 10:30am–12am. Tax refund available for purchases over ₩30,000. Korean fashion brands + international labels. Unlike wholesale markets, prices are fixed and cards accepted everywhere. Basement food court is solid.

Iconic traditional market — famous for bindaetteok (mung bean pancake), mayak gimbap (mini seaweed rice rolls), and yukhoe (beef tartare). CASH ONLY at food stalls. Bring ₩30,000–₩50,000 cash. Visit before 8pm — most food stalls close early. Sit anywhere, point at what you want, pay as you go.

Trendy Seongsu-dong curated shop complex. Rotating popup stores from Korean indie brands, cafes, and art spaces. Check their Instagram for current popup schedule — stores change every 2–4 weeks. Perfect for discovering emerging Korean fashion/beauty brands before they go mainstream.

Converted warehouse space hosting large-scale brand popups and exhibitions. Check Instagram for current event — sometimes free, sometimes ticketed. The space itself is architecturally stunning. Go on weekdays to avoid 2-hour weekend queues.

Seoul's tourist shopping mecca. Cosmetic stores have English-speaking staff and offer free samples at the door. Street food carts (cash only) line the main road after 4pm. Tax refund at most stores. Best for: K-beauty, fashion accessories, Korean snacks. Weekdays are less crowded than weekends.

Milan-meets-Seoul concept store in Cheongdam. Fashion, art, design, and a beautiful courtyard cafe. Tax refund available. More gallery than store — browse without pressure. The cafe alone is worth a visit. English spoken by staff.

Seoul's most famous vegan restaurant in Itaewon. Fully plant-based menu with incredible meat-substitute dishes — even non-vegans love it. English menu and English-speaking staff. The vegan burger and lasagna are standouts. Cash/card both accepted.

Vegan restaurant in Itaewon known for creative plant-based Korean fusion dishes. English-friendly with bilingual menu. The vegan dakgalbi (spicy stir-fried 'chicken') is their signature — made with soy protein. Popular with both vegans and curious carnivores.

Korea's only 3-Michelin-star restaurant. Chef Ahn Sung-jae (Culinary Class Wars judge). Tasting menu only — reserve via CatchTable exactly when slots open (1st of each month). Bookings sell out in seconds. English spoken. The most prestigious dining experience in Korea.

Korea's largest traditional market — 10,000+ stores. Best for: ginseng, dried seaweed, Korean snacks, cheap accessories. Most stalls are cash-only. Go in the morning for the best selection. The hotteok (sweet pancake) vendors near Gate 5 are legendary.

Unique market experience: buy a string of traditional coins (yeopjeon, ₩5,000) at the Dosirak Cafe, then trade them at participating stalls for small portions. Build your own Korean lunchbox. Cash only for coin purchase. Go before 2pm — stalls close early. Great for sampling many dishes at once.

Culinary Class Wars Black Spoon chef #1's restaurant in Myeongdong. Korean homestyle omakase — generous portions of banchan and stews. Korean-only service, but foreign tourists are welcome. Reserve via CatchTable. The galbi-jjim (braised short ribs) is the star.

Korean co-working chain with multiple Seoul locations. Daily pass ~₩25,000. Modern facilities with high-speed WiFi, free coffee, and meeting rooms. Seocho branch is spacious and quiet. Good for digital nomads wanting a desk for a few days without committing to a monthly plan.
